<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>auctio</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘increase’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>augere</ORIGIN> (➔ <REFHWD>AUGMENT</REFHWD>); because the money offered increases</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <LANG>Modern Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>elasticus</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Late Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>elastos</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘able to change shape’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Medieval Latin</LANG>, <LANG>Arabic</LANG> <ORIGIN>al-iksir</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘the elixir’</TRAN>, probably from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>xerion</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘powder for drying wounds’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>xeros</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘dry’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> From the idea of something suddenly <TRAN>‘emerging’</TRAN> or happening</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>empiric</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘person who puts trust only in practical experience’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(16-21 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>empiricus</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>empeiria</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘experience’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>engin</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘cleverness, machine’</TRAN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>ingenium</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘abilities you are born with’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>sconce</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘small strong building for defense’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(16-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Dutch</LANG> <ORIGIN>schans</ORIGIN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>entretenir</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to hold together, support’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>tenir</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to hold’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <LANG>German</LANG> <ORIGIN>enzym</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>zyme</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘substance that makes a flour-and-water mixture swell’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>epi-</ORIGIN> (➔ <REFHWD>EPICENTER</REFHWD>) + <ORIGIN>dura</ORIGIN>, from <ORIGIN>dura mater</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘outer covering of the main nerve in the back’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(14-21 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG>, <TRAN>‘hard mother’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <LANG>Medieval Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>epocha</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>epoche</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘ending, fixed point’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>epechein</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to stop for a time, hold back’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>aequare</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to make equal’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>aequus</ORIGIN>; ➔ <REFHWD>EQUAL</REFHWD><REFHOM>1</REFHOM></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Medieval Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>aequator</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘equalizer’</TRAN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>aequare</ORIGIN> (➔ <REFHWD>EQUATE</REFHWD>); because day and night are equal at the equator</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>équiper</ORIGIN>, probably from <LANG>Old Norse</LANG> <ORIGIN>skipa</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to make ready a ship’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>skip</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘ship’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <LANG>Late Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>aera</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘number for calculating from’</TRAN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG>, <TRAN>‘counters’</TRAN>, plural of <ORIGIN>aes</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘copper, money’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>eradicare</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to pull out by the root’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>radix</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘root’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>eradere</ORIGIN>, from <ORIGIN>radere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to rub roughly, scrape’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1100-1200</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> perhaps from <LANG>Medieval Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>mus Armenius</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘Armenian mouse’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>erudire</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to give instruction to’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>rudis</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘rude, uneducated’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1200-1300</CENTURY> <LANG>Old North French</LANG> <ORIGIN>escaper</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Vulgar Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>excappare</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Late Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>cappa</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘head-covering’</TRAN>; from the idea of throwing off something that limits your movement</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1200-1300</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>eschete</ORIGIN>, from <ORIGIN>escheoir</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to fall, be given to another’</TRAN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>cadere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to fall’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>oisophagos</ORIGIN>, from <ORIGIN>oisein</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to be going to carry’</TRAN> + <ORIGIN>phagein</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to eat’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <LANG>Dutch</LANG> <ORIGIN>etsen</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>German</LANG> <ORIGIN>ätzen</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to feed’</TRAN>; because originally the lines were <TRAN>‘eaten’</TRAN> into the metal with acid</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>ethane</ORIGIN> name of a type of gas <CENTURY>(19-21 centuries)</CENTURY> (from <ORIGIN>ethyl</ORIGIN>) + <ORIGIN>-ol</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘type of alcohol’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> ethyl from <ORIGIN>ether</ORIGIN> + <ORIGIN>-yl</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘type of group of atoms’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <LANG>Modern Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>eu-</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘well, good’</TRAN> (from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>ey</ORIGIN>, <ORIGIN>eu</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘good’</TRAN>) + <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>kalyptos</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘covered’</TRAN>; from the covering on the tree's buds</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>eunuchus</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>eunouchos</ORIGIN>, from <ORIGIN>eune</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘bed’</TRAN> + <ORIGIN>echein</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to be in charge of’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1100-1200</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>evangeliste</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>euangelion</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘good news, gospel’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>eu-</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘good’</TRAN> + <ORIGIN>angelos</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘someone who brings a message’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>exigere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to drive out, demand, measure’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>agere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to drive’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>exaggerare</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to make into a pile’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>agger</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘pile’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>excerpere</ORIGIN>, from <ORIGIN>carpere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to gather, pick’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>excidere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to cut out’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>caedere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to cut’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>excrescens</ORIGIN>, present participle of <ORIGIN>excrescere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to grow out’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>crescere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to grow’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>excretus</ORIGIN>, past participle of <ORIGIN>excernere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to separate out’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>cernere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to separate’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>exécuter</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>exsecutus</ORIGIN>, past participle of <ORIGIN>exsequi</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to execute’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>sequi</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to follow’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>exsistere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to come into being, exist’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>sistere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to stand’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>exspatiatus</ORIGIN>, past paticiple of <ORIGIN>exspatiari</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to wander freely’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>spatium</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘space’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <LANG>Medieval Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>expatriare</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>patria</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘your own country’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>exspectare</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to look forward to’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>spectare</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to look at’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>expedire</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to free the feet, set free, arrange’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>pes</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘foot’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <LANG>Late Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>expletivus</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>explere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to fill out’</TRAN>; because the words fill a space in a sentence without adding to the meaning</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Medieval Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>expropriare</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>proprius</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘your own’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>expungere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to mark something with small holes to show that it is to be removed’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>pungere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to make a small hole’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>exquisitus</ORIGIN>, past participle of <ORIGIN>exquirere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to search out’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>quaerere</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to look for’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>exstans</ORIGIN>, present participle of <ORIGIN>exstare</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to stand out, exist’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>stare</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to stand’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>exterminare</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to drive out’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>terminus</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘edge’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Old North French</LANG> <ORIGIN>garser</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>charassein</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to make a mark in the surface of something’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>gaud</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘bright decorative object’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(14-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, probably from <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>gaudir</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to enjoy’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> Perhaps from <ORIGIN>gaze</ORIGIN> + <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>-ebo</ORIGIN> (as in videbo <TRAN>‘I shall see’</TRAN>)</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG>, <LANG>Italian</LANG> <ORIGIN>gazzetta</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Italian dialect</LANG> <ORIGIN>gazeta</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘small coin’</TRAN> (the price of the newspaper)</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> From The Gazetteer's: or, Newsman's Interpreter (1693), a dictionary of place-names by Laurence Echard, from <ORIGIN>gazetteer</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘newspaper writer’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(17-19 centuries)</CENTURY></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> Probably from <LANG>Scottish English</LANG> <ORIGIN>guiser</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘person dressed in something that hides who they are’</TRAN>, from <LANG>English</LANG> <ORIGIN>guise</ORIGIN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>generare</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to produce children’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>genus</ORIGIN>; ➔ <REFHWD>GENUS</REFHWD></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>généreux</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>generosus</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘born into a high rank’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>genus</ORIGIN>; ➔ <REFHWD>GENUS</REFHWD></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <TRAN>‘spirit who guards a person or place’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>gignere</ORIGIN>; ➔ <REFHWD>GENITAL</REFHWD></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>gentiana</ORIGIN>, perhaps from <ORIGIN>Gentius</ORIGIN> king of ancient Illyria, in Southern Europe, who is said to have discovered the use of the plant as a medicine</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1200-1300</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>gentil</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>gentilis</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘of a family, of the same family’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>gens</ORIGIN>; ➔ <REFHWD>GENTILE</REFHWD></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG>, <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>geranion</ORIGIN>, from <ORIGIN>geranos</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘crane’</TRAN>; because the plant's seed-case looks like a crane's long beak</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>gerbille</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>gerboa</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘jerboa’</TRAN>, a type of small animal, from <LANG>Arabic</LANG> <ORIGIN>yarbu'</ORIGIN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> past participle of <ORIGIN>germinare</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to start to grow’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>germen</ORIGIN>; ➔ <REFHWD>GERM</REFHWD></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> Elbridge <ORIGIN>Gerry</ORIGIN> (1744-1818), US politician + <ORIGIN>salamander</ORIGIN>; because a voting area he made to help his own party win an election was said to be shaped like a salamander</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <LANG>Icelandic</LANG> <ORIGIN>Geysir</ORIGIN>, name of a hot spring in Iceland, from <ORIGIN>geysa</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to flow out quickly’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <LANG>Italian</LANG> perhaps from <ORIGIN>getto</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘place where metals are melted and made into things’</TRAN>; because a 16th-century ghetto in Venice was where such a place had once existed</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>gibelet</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘dish made from the organs of hunted animals’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>gigolette</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘girl who goes to dances, prostitute’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>gigue</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘dance’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>guimbelet</ORIGIN>, probably from <ORIGIN>Middle Dutch</ORIGIN> <ORIGIN>wimmel</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘tool for making holes’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>geneva</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘gin’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(18-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Dutch</LANG> <ORIGIN>genever</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>juniperus</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘juniper’</TRAN>, plant used to give gin its taste</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1200-1300</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>gingebras</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘preserved ginger’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(13-14 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>gingembraz</ORIGIN>, from <ORIGIN>gimgibre</ORIGIN> (➔ <REFHWD>GINGER</REFHWD>); influenced by <ORIGIN>bread</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> Perhaps from <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>gensor</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘more small and graceful’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>gent</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘born into high rank, small and graceful’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> Based on <ORIGIN>Man Friday</ORIGIN>, a character in the book Robinson Crusoe (1719) who does jobs for the main character, Robinson Crusoe</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>gurle</ORIGIN>, <ORIGIN>girle</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘child, young person’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(13-15 centuries)</CENTURY>, of unknown origin</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <LANG>Anglo-French</LANG> <TRAN>‘it lies, it can be presented in a court of law’</TRAN>, from <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>gesir</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to lie’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>get</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘someone born to an unmarried mother’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(16-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>get</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to beget’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Old North French</LANG> <ORIGIN>guisier</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>gigeria</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘inside organs of animals’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <LANG>Scottish English</LANG> <TRAN>‘magic’</TRAN>, from <LANG>English</LANG> <ORIGIN>grammar</ORIGIN>; because of an old association of knowledge with magic</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> Perhaps from <ORIGIN>glent</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to move quickly, shine’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(13-19 centuries)</CENTURY></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> Probably from <LANG>Scottish English</LANG> <ORIGIN>glaum</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to seize’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(18-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Scottish Gaelic</LANG> <ORIGIN>glam</ORIGIN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> Probably from the sound it makes</SENSE>

<SENSE><sensenum>1-2, 5-6 </sensenum><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> Probably from a <LANG>Scandinavian</LANG> language. </SENSE><SENSE><sensenum>3-4 </sensenum><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>glose</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>glossa</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘unusual word that needs explaining’</TRAN>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG>, <TRAN>‘tongue, language, unusual word’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>glum</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to look annoyed or bored’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(15-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>gloom</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>knurled</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘having raised lines on the surface’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(17-21 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>knurl</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘small part sticking out’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(17-21 centuries)</CENTURY></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>gobelin</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Medieval Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>gobelinus</ORIGIN>, perhaps from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>kobalos</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘bad man’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>God speed you</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘may God give you success’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> Perhaps from an unrecorded <ORIGIN>gog</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to move up and down’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> From a trick in which a brick is sold by pretending it is made of gold</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> Perhaps from <LANG>Middle Dutch</LANG> <ORIGIN>colf</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘stick for hitting a ball’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> Probably from <ORIGIN>goff</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘stupid person’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(16-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>goffe</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘awkward, stupid’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> Partly from <ORIGIN>gooney</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘stupid person’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(16-19 centuries)</CENTURY>; partly from Alice the <ORIGIN>Goon</ORIGIN>, character invented by the U.S. cartoonist E. C. Segar</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>gorgias</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘stylish’</TRAN>, perhaps from <ORIGIN>gorgias</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘cloth tied around the neck’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>gorge</ORIGIN>; ➔ <REFHWD>GORGE</REFHWD><REFHOM>1</REFHOM></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>Gorillai</ORIGIN>, name of an African tribe of hairy women in old stories</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>gaumless</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘lacking understanding’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(18-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>gaum</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘understanding’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(18-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old Norse</LANG> <ORIGIN>gaumr</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘heed, attention’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>goose</ORIGIN> + <ORIGIN>summer</ORIGIN>; probably because gossamer appears at a time of year when geese are ready for eating</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1200-1300</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>goute</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>gutta</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘drop’</TRAN>; because it used to be believed that it was caused by drops of disease in the blood</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1200-1300</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>governer</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>gubernare</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>kybernan</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to control the direction of something’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>graff</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘graft’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(14-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>grafe</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘pencil, graft’</TRAN>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>graphein</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to write’</TRAN>; because a plant graft looks like a pencil</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>gramme</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>gramma</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘letter, writing, small weight’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>graphein</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to write’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> Because it grows in bunches, like grapes</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <LANG>German</LANG> <ORIGIN>graphit</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>graphein</ORIGIN> (➔ <REFHWD>-GRAPHY</REFHWD>); because it is used in pencils</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>grater</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to make marks in a surface’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Medieval Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>crata</ORIGIN>, <ORIGIN>grata</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘something made of woven sticks’</TRAN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>cratis</ORIGIN>; ➔ <REFHWD>CRATE</REFHWD><REFHOM>1</REFHOM></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> Probably because it was originally painted green</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> Perhaps from <LANG>Irish Gaelic</LANG> <ORIGIN>gruaimin</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘angry little man’</TRAN>; influenced by <ORIGIN>goblin</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG> <TRAN>‘pomegranate, grenade’</TRAN>, from <LANG>Late Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>granata</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>granum</ORIGIN> (➔ <REFHWD>GRAIN</REFHWD>); because the bomb looks like the fruit</SENSE>
<SENSE><LANG>Old English</LANG> <TRAN>‘grain to be broken into small pieces’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> Perhaps from <ORIGIN>Grizel</ORIGIN>, a form of the name <ORIGIN>Griselda</ORIGIN>; <TRAN>‘patient Griselda’</TRAN> was a well-known name for an obedient wife</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1200-1300</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>grossier</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘person who sells in large quantities’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>gros</ORIGIN>; ➔ <REFHWD>GROSS</REFHWD><REFHOM>1</REFHOM></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>Old Grog</ORIGIN>, a name given (because he wore a coat of <ORIGIN>grogram</ORIGIN>, a type of rough cloth) to Edward Vernon, the 18th-century British navy officer who started the practice of giving sailors rum and water to drink</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> From early <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>gormette</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘one of the leather bands around a horse's head’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><sensenum>1 </sensenum><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>➔ <REFHWD>BRIDEGROOM</REFHWD></ORIGIN></SENSE><SENSE><sensenum>2 </sensenum><CENTURY>1100-1200</CENTURY> Probably from an unrecorded <LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>groma</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘boy, man, male servant’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> Probably from <ORIGIN>grutch</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘complaint’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(14-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>grouchier</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to complain’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> Perhaps from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>gruta</ORIGIN>, name of a type of bird hunted for food</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>groveling</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘lying face downward’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(16-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>groof</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘on the face’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(14-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old Norse</LANG> <ORIGIN>grufu</ORIGIN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> Probably from ➔ <REFHWD>GRUB</REFHWD><REFHOM>1</REFHOM> 2; in sense 1, probably because birds eat grubs</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>➔ <REFHWD>GRUB</REFHWD><REFHOM>1</REFHOM></ORIGIN>; because places full of grubs are dirty</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>gruel</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to punish’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(1800-1900)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>gruel</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘food’</TRAN>; because people were given gruel as a punishment</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>grump</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘grumpy person’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(18-21 centuries)</CENTURY>, probably from the sound of someone complaining</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> Probably from the sound of someone talking nonsense</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG> from Joseph <ORIGIN>Guillotin</ORIGIN> (1738-1814), French doctor who invented it</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>Guinea</ORIGIN>, probably by mistake for <ORIGIN>Guiana</ORIGIN>, earlier name of <ORIGIN>Guyana</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>guitare</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Spanish</LANG> <ORIGIN>guitarra</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Arabic</LANG> <ORIGIN>qitar</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>kithara</ORIGIN> type of stringed instrument</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> <LANG>Russian</LANG> <ORIGIN>Glavnoe Upravlenie Ispravitel'no-trudovykh Lagerei</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘Chief Directorate of Labor Camps’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>gumshoe</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘rubber shoe’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(19-20 centuries)</CENTURY>; because detectives walk quietly</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> Perhaps from <ORIGIN>Gunnilda</ORIGIN>, a woman's name, from <LANG>Old Norse</LANG> <ORIGIN>Gunnhildr</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> <LANG>Chinese</LANG> <ORIGIN>gonghe</ORIGIN>, from <ORIGIN>jongguo gongye hozo she</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘Chinese Industrial Cooperatives Society’</TRAN>, used as a battle cry (meaning "work together") by U.S. soldiers in World War II</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> Perhaps suggesting stickiness, or perhaps from <ORIGIN>goo</ORIGIN> + <ORIGIN>sponge</ORIGIN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>Gunk</ORIGIN>, a trademark for a type of soap</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>gun</ORIGIN> + <ORIGIN>wale</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘band’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(11-20 centuries)</CENTURY> (from <LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>walu</ORIGIN>); because it was once used to support guns</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> R. J. L. <ORIGIN>Guppy</ORIGIN> (1836-1916), Trinidadian priest who discovered it</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>Gurney cab</ORIGIN> <CENTURY>(19-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, a type of horse-drawn vehicle invented by J. T. <ORIGIN>Gurney</ORIGIN> of Boston, Massachusetts</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> Perhaps from <ORIGIN>gussie</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘weak or stupid man’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(19-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, from the male or female name <ORIGIN>Gussie</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><sensenum>1-2, 4-5 </sensenum><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>Guy</ORIGIN> Fawkes (1570-1606), who tried to blow up the English parliament. </SENSE><SENSE><sensenum>3 </sensenum><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> Probably from <LANG>Dutch</LANG> <ORIGIN>gei</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘type of rope’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> Perhaps from <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>gosillier</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to talk a lot, vomit’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG>, <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>gymnasion</ORIGIN>, from <ORIGIN>gymnazein</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to exercise with no clothes on’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>gymnos</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘naked’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG>, <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>gyros</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘ring, circle’</TRAN>; because it was originally used to show how the Earth turns</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <TRAN>‘you should have the body’</TRAN> (the first words on the document which demands that the person should be allowed to come to court)</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG>, <LANG>Latin</LANG>, from <ORIGIN>habitare</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to live in a place’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>habere</ORIGIN>; ➔ <REFHWD>HABIT</REFHWD></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> Probably from an unrecorded <LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>hacule</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘small hook’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>hackney</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to use (a horse) for ordinary riding, to use (something) too much’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(16-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>hackney</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘horse for ordinary riding’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(14-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, probably from <ORIGIN>Hackney</ORIGIN>, area in London, England where horses were once kept</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> Perhaps from an unrecorded <ORIGIN>hakefish</ORIGIN>, from <ORIGIN>hake</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘hook’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(15-19 centuries)</CENTURY></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>halcyon</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘bird believed to bring good weather at sea’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(14-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>alkyon</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘kingfisher’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <LANG>Late Latin</LANG> present participle of <ORIGIN>itinerari</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to go on a journey’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>iter</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘journey’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>job</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to jab’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(15-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, probably copying the action</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>Jack</ORIGIN> male name + <ORIGIN>daw</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘jackdaw’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(15-19 centuries)</CENTURY> (perhaps from an unrecorded <LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>dawe</ORIGIN>)</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>jag</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘cut in the edge of a piece of cloth’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(14-18 centuries)</CENTURY>, perhaps from <LANG>Arabic</LANG> <ORIGIN>az-zagaye</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘the lance’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>jape</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to joke, make fun of someone’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(14-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>japer</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to cry out like a dog’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> Early <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>javeline</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>javelot</ORIGIN>, probably from a <LANG>Celtic</LANG> language</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1200-1300</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG>, <LANG>Late Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>gaius</ORIGIN>, probably from the name <ORIGIN>Gaius</ORIGIN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> Perhaps from <LANG>Bantu</LANG> <ORIGIN>jas</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to cause to dance, excite’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>jean</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘strong cotton cloth’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(15-21 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>Gene</ORIGIN>, early form of the name <ORIGIN>Genoa</ORIGIN>, Italian city where the cloth was first made</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>jejunus</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘not eating, (of food) small in amount’</TRAN>; influenced in meaning by <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>jeune</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘young’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><sensenum>1-2 </sensenum><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>➔ <REFHWD>JET</REFHWD><REFHOM>2</REFHOM></ORIGIN></SENSE><SENSE><sensenum>3 </sensenum><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>jaiet</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>gagates</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Greek</LANG>, from <ORIGIN>Gagas</ORIGIN>, town and river in Asia Minor where it was found</SENSE>

<SENSE><sensenum>1 </sensenum><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> Origin unknown. </SENSE><SENSE><sensenum>2 </sensenum><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> Perhaps from <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>giber</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to shake’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>jingo</ORIGIN> <CENTURY>(17-21 centuries)</CENTURY> (probably from <ORIGIN>Jesus</ORIGIN>), used in the phrase by jingo as an exclamation in a 19th-century British song encouraging people to fight for their country</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1900-2000</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>jitter</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to move quickly and nervously’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(1900-2000)</CENTURY> (from <ORIGIN>jitters</ORIGIN>) + <ORIGIN>bug</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘person with a strong interest in something’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(19-21 centuries)</CENTURY>; probably invented by the U.S. band leader Cab Calloway</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>joss</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘Chinese figure of a god, luck’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(18-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Pidgin English</LANG>, from <LANG>Portuguese</LANG> <ORIGIN>deus</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘god’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>jubilé</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Late Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>jubilaeus</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Late Greek</LANG> <ORIGIN>iobelaios</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Hebrew</LANG> <ORIGIN>yobhel</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘sheep's horn, jubilee’</TRAN>; influenced by <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>jubilare</ORIGIN> (➔ <REFHWD>JUBILANT</REFHWD>)</SENSE>

<SENSE><sensenum>1-2 </sensenum><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> Probably from <ORIGIN>jump</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘loose coat’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(17-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, probably from <ORIGIN>jupe</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘coat’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(13-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old French</LANG>, from <LANG>Arabic</LANG> <ORIGIN>jubbah</ORIGIN></SENSE><SENSE><sensenum>3 </sensenum><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>➔ <REFHWD>JUMP</REFHWD><REFHOM>1</REFHOM></ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>junket</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘sweet food made from milk’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(15-21 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old North French</LANG> <ORIGIN>jonquette</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘basket made from reeds’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>jonc</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘reed’</TRAN>; because the food was made or served in such baskets</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> From an unrecorded <LANG>Old North French</LANG> <ORIGIN>kenil</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Vulgar Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>canile</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>canis</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘dog’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>scrow</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘scroll’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(13-17 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>escroue</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘piece of paper, scroll’</TRAN>; influenced by <ORIGIN>roll</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>shamble</ORIGIN> (of legs) <TRAN>‘curved, badly formed’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(16-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, probably from <ORIGIN>shamble</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘table from which meat is sold’</TRAN> (➔ <REFHWD>SHAMBLES</REFHWD>); probably because of the similarity to table legs</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> Perhaps from <LANG>Scottish English</LANG>, <TRAN>‘person who cannot see well’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1100-1200</CENTURY> <LANG>Anglo-French</LANG> <ORIGIN>suer</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to follow, make a legal claim to’</TRAN>, from <LANG>Vulgar Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>sequere</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>sequi</ORIGIN>; ➔ <REFHWD>SEQUENCE</REFHWD></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>sirly</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘like a lord, proud and grand’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(14-17 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>sir</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <LANG>French</LANG> <ORIGIN>suzerainté</ORIGIN>, from an unrecorded <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>suserain</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘ruler’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>sus</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘up’</TRAN> + <ORIGIN>-erain</ORIGIN> (as in <ORIGIN>soverain</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘sovereign’</TRAN>)</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> Probably from <ORIGIN>sweddle</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to swaddle’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(13-17 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>swethel</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘swaddling clothes’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>swag</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to sway, hang down’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(16-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, probably from a <LANG>Scandinavian</LANG> language</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>swank</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to behave showily and boastfully’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(19-21 centuries)</CENTURY>, perhaps from <LANG>Middle High German</LANG> <ORIGIN>swanken</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to move unsteadily from one side to another’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>swap</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to hit’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(14-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from the sound; from the practice of striking the hands together when agreeing a business deal</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>swarty</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘swarthy’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(16-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>swart</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘dark, swarthy’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(11-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>sweart</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>swashbuckler</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘noisy boastful fighter’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(16-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>swash</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to wave around or hit noisily’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(15-20 centuries)</CENTURY> (probably from the sound) + <ORIGIN>buckler</ORIGIN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>squat</ORIGIN> in its original meaning <TRAN>‘to crush’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(13-19 centuries)</CENTURY></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>sweepstake</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘someone who takes all the winnings in a game’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(15-18 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>sweep</ORIGIN> + <ORIGIN>stake</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>swelt</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to die, become unconscious because of heat’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(11-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>sweltan</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to die’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>swindler</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘person who swindles’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(18-21 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>German</LANG> <ORIGIN>schwindler</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘someone confused or unbalanced’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> Perhaps from <LANG>German</LANG> <ORIGIN>wedeln</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to wag the tail, be willing to do what others want’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> From the sound of a light movement of air carrying a smell</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>whiskers</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘mustache’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(16-20 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>whisker</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘something that whisks or sweeps’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(15-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>whisk</ORIGIN>; because the mustache looks like a small brush</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <LANG>Irish Gaelic</LANG> <ORIGIN>uisce beathadh</ORIGIN> and <LANG>Scottish Gaelic</LANG> <ORIGIN>uisge beatha</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘water of life’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>whisk</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘whist’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(17-19 centuries)</CENTURY>; perhaps because the cards are <TRAN>‘whisked’</TRAN> or taken away quickly</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> From the supposed practice of the King of Siam, who gave to people he did not like a white elephant, which cost a very large amount of money to keep</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>whittle</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘large knife’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(15-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>thwittle</ORIGIN> <CENTURY>(14-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>thwite</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to whittle’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(11-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>thwitan</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>periwig</ORIGIN> type of wig <CENTURY>(16-21 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>perruque</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Old Italian</LANG> <ORIGIN>perrucca</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘hair, wig’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1200-1300</CENTURY> Probably from <LANG>Middle Dutch</LANG> or <LANG>Middle Low German</LANG> <ORIGIN>wiggelen</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to walk unsteadily’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>wildeornes</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘land where wild animals live’</TRAN>, from <ORIGIN>wild deor</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘wild animal’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1100-1200</CENTURY> Perhaps from an unrecorded <LANG>Old North French</LANG> <ORIGIN>wile</ORIGIN> (singular), from <LANG>Old French</LANG> <ORIGIN>guile</ORIGIN>; ➔ <REFHWD>GUILE</REFHWD></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>will I nill I</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘(whether) I am willing (or) I am unwilling’</TRAN>; nill <TRAN>‘to be unwilling’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(11-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>nyllan</ORIGIN>, from <ORIGIN>ne</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘not’</TRAN> + <ORIGIN>wyllan</ORIGIN> (➔ <REFHWD>WILL</REFHWD><REFHOM>1</REFHOM>)</SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1200-1300</CENTURY> <LANG>Old North French</LANG> <ORIGIN>wenchier</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to be impatient, move about suddenly’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> Because the large sails <TRAN>‘jam’</TRAN> or block the wind</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>wistly</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘with close attention’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(15-18 centuries)</CENTURY>, probably from <ORIGIN>whist</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘quiet’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(15-20 centuries)</CENTURY>; influenced by <ORIGIN>wishful</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1700-1800</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>witch-hazel</ORIGIN> type of elm tree <CENTURY>(16-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>witch</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘tree with bendy branches’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(11-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>wice</ORIGIN>, <ORIGIN>wic</ORIGIN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> Probably from <ORIGIN>weather</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to affect with sun, wind, rain, etc.’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1200-1300</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>woe</ORIGIN> + <ORIGIN>begone</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘affected’</TRAN> (from <ORIGIN>bego</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to surround, affect’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(11-16 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>began</ORIGIN>, from <ORIGIN>gan</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to go’</TRAN>)</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1400-1500</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>wonders</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘wondrous’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(13-17 centuries)</CENTURY>, from the possessive form of <ORIGIN>wonder</ORIGIN>; influenced by <ORIGIN>marvelous</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1100-1200</CENTURY> Past participle of <ORIGIN>wone</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to be used to doing something’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(11-17 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>wunian</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to live in a place, be used to’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> Because originally the instruments were all made of wood</SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1800-1900</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>workyday</ORIGIN> <CENTURY>(16-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>workyday</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘working day’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(16-19 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>workeday</ORIGIN> <CENTURY>(12-16 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <ORIGIN>work</ORIGIN> + <ORIGIN>day</ORIGIN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> Perhaps from <LANG>Low German</LANG> <ORIGIN>wrangeln</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to fight, make a disturbance’</TRAN></SENSE>
<SENSE><CENTURY>1500-1600</CENTURY> Partly from <ORIGIN>wreath</ORIGIN>; partly from <ORIGIN>wrethen</ORIGIN>, old past participle of <ORIGIN>writhe</ORIGIN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1300-1400</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>wrinkled</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘twisted, winding’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(14-16 centuries)</CENTURY>, probably from <LANG>Old English</LANG> <ORIGIN>gewrinclian</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘to wind’</TRAN></SENSE>

<SENSE><CENTURY>1600-1700</CENTURY> <ORIGIN>wyver</ORIGIN> <TRAN>‘viper, wyvern’</TRAN> <CENTURY>(14-17 centuries)</CENTURY>, from <LANG>Old North French</LANG> <ORIGIN>wivre</ORIGIN>, from <LANG>Latin</LANG> <ORIGIN>vipera</ORIGIN>; ➔ <REFHWD>VIPER</REFHWD></SENSE>
